food_subgroup: Japanese sweet dumpling dessert with azuki bean paste or soup

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one bowl (200 g) containing several shiratama dumplings in azuki bean soup or topped with sweet azuki paste, as typically served in Japanese dessert restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Mochi (glutinous rice cake) and Anko (sweet red bean paste) composite estimate; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Glutinous Rice (NDB 20060) and Red Beans cooked (NDB 16014)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for azuki shiratama as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked glutinous rice (NDB 20060) and cooked adzuki beans (NDB 16014) combined in approximate proportions typical of shiratama dessert: ~50% shiratama dumplings (glutinous rice flour + water, boiled) and ~50% sweet azuki bean soup/paste (azuki beans + sugar + water). Sugar addition for anko estimated at ~15 g per 100 g of final dish. Composite macros calculated by weighted average.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; Glutinous rice / mochi analogous estimate; Adzuki bean GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for azuki shiratama as a composite dish. GI estimated as weighted average of two components: (1) Shiratama dumplings (glutinous rice flour, boiled) — GI approximately 65 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice/mochi; (2) Azuki bean component — GI approximately 35 per University of Sydney GI Database (adzuki beans, boiled). Approximate 50:50 carbohydrate contribution from each component yields blended GI estimate of ~48. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 65.0 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 = 31.2. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~8 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Azuki shiratama is very low in sodium (~16 mg per 200 g serving), making it a suitable occasional dessert for individuals managing hypertension, provided added salt or high-sodium toppings are avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low-to-moderate GI; sugar content from anko (sweet azuki paste)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 48 and a glycemic load of approximately 31 per 200 g serving, azuki shiratama poses a moderate glycemic concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control is advised and the azuki bean component provides dietary fibre (~5.6 g per serving) which may attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and cholesterol; azuki beans provide beneficial fibre and polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Azuki shiratama is very low in saturated fat (~0.2 g per 200 g serving) and contains no cholesterol, and the azuki bean component contributes dietary fibre and polyphenolic antioxidants that may support cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Azuki Shiratama (小豆白玉) is a traditional Japanese dessert consisting of soft, chewy shiratama dumplings made from shiratamako (glutinous rice flour) served with sweet azuki bean soup (shiruko) or topped with sweet azuki bean paste (anko). Shiratama dumplings are formed by kneading glutinous rice flour with water into small round balls and boiling them until they float, yielding a smooth, mochi-like texture that is soft, slightly elastic, and mildly flavoured. The azuki bean component — either as a thick sweet paste or a thinner sweet soup — provides a rich, earthy sweetness with a slightly grainy texture. This dessert is popular in Japanese wagashi (traditional confectionery) culture and is commonly served in Japanese tea houses, dessert cafes, and Japanese restaurants. Nutritionally, azuki shiratama is moderate in calories (~148 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides a meaningful amount of dietary fibre (~2.8 g per 100 g) primarily from the azuki beans. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 48 (low GI), though the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is moderate (~31) due to the carbohydrate density. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise portion control. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and very low in sodium, making it broadly suitable across many dietary requirements. It is also a source of plant-based iron, folate, potassium, and magnesium from the azuki beans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for azuki shiratama as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for glutinous rice and cooked adzuki beans in approximate proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the source database. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this plant-based dessert. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dessert with no animal fats or partially hydrogenated oils; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. Cholesterol (cholesterol_mg) is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. The food_name 'Azuki Bean Shiratama Dumpling Dessert' is used as the canonical English name; the field survey menu name '902D Azuki Shiratama(D)' is preserved as an alias. The '(D)' suffix in the original menu name likely denotes a dessert category code used by the restaurant GyoGyo - Funan. Nutrient values should be verified against direct laboratory analysis or a more specific approved source if available.
